channel.title: NYT > Arts
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Highlights From the 2026 Oscars: ‘One Battle After Another’ Wins Six Awards, Including Best Picture
item.description: Paul Thomas Anderson’s film also took best director and best supporting actor for Sean Penn. Michael B. Jordan won best actor for “Sinners,” and Jessie Buckley was named best actress for “Hamnet.”
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: Paul Thomas Anderson, at microphone, accepts the best picture Oscar for “One Battle After Another.”

item.title: Our Favorite Oscars Red Carpet Photos Featuring Jessie Buckley and Emma Stone
item.description: Grand entrances, statement-making fashion and the occasional candid moment, on a night geared for gold.
item.dc:creator: Sinna Nasseri
item.media:credit: Sinna Nasseri for The New York Times
item.media:description: Teyana Taylor, nominated for best supporting actress in “One Battle After Another.”

item.title: Oscars 2026 Winners List: ‘One Battle After Another,’ ‘Sinners’ and More
item.description: The winning actors, directors, pictures and more at the 98th annual Academy Awards.
item.dc:creator: Shivani Gonzalez
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: Michael B. Jordan, left, and Jessie Buckley, right, who took home the lead acting Oscars.

item.title: Brené Brown and Adam Grant Want to Repair the Discourse
item.description: With a new video podcast, the influential authors and former nemeses aim to inject a dose of rationality and humility into your algorithm.
item.dc:creator: Reggie Ugwu
item.media:credit: Chris Pizzello/Invision, via Associated Press; Erik Tanner for The New York Times
item.media:description: Brené Brown and Adam Grant are academics and public intellectuals, known for best-selling books and viral TED Talks.

item.title: Why Are We in Iran? On ‘S.N.L.’, Pete Hegseth Has the Answer: ‘I Don’t Know’
item.description: Harry Styles is host and musical guest, while James Austin Johnson and Colin Jost cement their duo as President Trump and his defense secretary on “Saturday Night Live.”
item.dc:creator: Dave Itzkoff

item.title: ‘Trash’ Review: Bickering About Chores, in Sign Language
item.description: James Caverly and Andrew Morrill star as Deaf roommates in their new comedy at the Perelman Performing Arts Center.
item.dc:creator: Laura Collins-Hughes
item.category: Perelman, Ronald O, Performing Arts Center
item.media:credit: Rachel Papo for The New York Times
item.media:description: From left, James Caverly, Andrew Morrill and Chris Ogren in “Trash” at Perelman Performing Arts Center in Manhattan.

item.title: The City That Inspired Rothko (It’s Not New York)
item.description: What the American painter saw during his trips to Florence molded his vision and his understanding of space and color.
item.dc:creator: Elisabetta Povoledo
item.media:credit: Roberto Serra - Iguana Press/Getty Images

item.title: Gorillaz on ‘The Mountain’ and Celebrating Lost Musicians
item.description: “We need young artists to engage with other things than fame and image and likes,” Damon Albarn said. The band’s latest album proves the point.
item.dc:creator: Brian Raftery
item.media:credit: OK McCausland for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jamie Hewlett and Damon Albarn’s latest Gorillaz album features voices of lost former collaborators.

item.title: Kacey Musgraves, the Pussycat Dolls: 8 Songs We’re Talking About This Week
item.description: Kacey Musgraves laments her frustration, the Pussycat Dolls attempt a comeback and Bella Kay cracks the Top 40 with a tune about turmoil.
item.dc:creator: Jon Pareles
item.media:credit: Seth Herald/Reuters
item.media:description: Kacey Musgraves announced a new album, “Middle of Nowhere,” with a song filled with not-so-subtle references.

item.title: Coalition of States Will Carry on Live Nation Antitrust Case
item.description: The federal government has settled its claims, but dozens of states on Monday will resume a trial that accuses the company of being a monopoly.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario
item.media:credit: Patrick T. Fallon/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Live Nation reached a settlement with the Justice Department that did not satisfy the attorneys general of many states.

item.title: On the Road, Primal Screams Optional, With Mae Martin
item.description: “It’s changed my whole personality,” the comedian said of getting a driver’s license. They are now on a 37-city tour, though someone else is driving.
item.dc:creator: Maya Salam
item.media:credit: Jutharat Pinyodoonyachet for The New York Times

item.title: Kennedy Center’s President, Richard Grenell, Is Leaving After a Tumultuous Year
item.description: Since Richard Grenell was appointed by President Trump, the arts center has endured waves of cancellations and departures. It will soon close for lengthy renovations.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nagourney and Julia Jacobs
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Valerie Plesch for The New York Times
item.media:description: During Richard Grenell’s tenure as president of the Kennedy Center, the Washington National Opera announced it would find a new home.

item.title: Crisis Follows Conductor’s Dismissal at the Boston Symphony
item.description: A decision by the orchestra’s board to fire its music director, Andris Nelsons, has set off protests from players, leaving their leaders scrambling.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nagourney

item.title: Five Science Fiction Movies to Stream Now
item.description: In this month’s picks, there are time-looping teenagers, a dermatologist’s nightmare and a story from the alien’s perspective.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli

item.title: Why Did Trump Officials Award $2 Million to a Small Art School in Queens?
item.description: The National Endowment for the Humanities seldom gave seven-figure grants. Now big awards flow to handpicked projects, including an institution with three full-time employees.
item.dc:creator: Zachary Small, Jennifer Schuessler and Marcus Maddox
item.media:description: Jacob Collins, the founder and artistic director of Grand Central Atelier, has had a lifetime mission to inspire a return to a classical style of art.

item.title: ‘Imperfect Women,’ Plus 7 Things to Watch on TV This Week
item.description: A new show starring Elisabeth Moss and Kerry Washington premieres. And Taylor Frankie Paul leads the new season of “The Bachelorette.”
item.dc:creator: Shivani Gonzalez
item.media:credit: Courtesy of Apple TV.
item.media:description: Elisabeth Moss, left, and Kerry Washington in “Imperfect Women.”

item.title: Javier Bardem and Other Stars Address Wars and Free Speech at Oscars
item.description: Conan O’Brien, the ceremony’s host, noted in his opening monologue that viewers were “all too aware that these are very chaotic, frightening times.”
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jimmy Kimmel delivered one of the sharpest political jokes of Oscars night while introducing the documentary awards.

item.title: ‘One Battle After Another’ Wins Best Picture at 2026 Oscars After Awards Sweep
item.description: The epic about radicals and reactionaries also took best director for Paul Thomas Anderson, his first Academy Awards after 11 losses.
item.dc:creator: Kyle Buchanan
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: Paul Thomas Anderson and the producer Sara Murphy accepted the Oscar for best picture as cast and crew members watched.

item.title: Jessie Buckley Wins First Oscar for Best Actress in ‘Hamnet’
item.description: The actress closes out a season of wins in this category with an Academy Award.
item.dc:creator: Esther Zuckerman
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jessie Buckley accepting her best actress Oscar for “Hamnet.”

item.title: Michael B. Jordan Wins the Best Actor Oscar for ‘Sinners’
item.description: It was the actor’s first nomination for an Academy Award.
item.dc:creator: Maya Salam
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: Michael B. Jordan, who played twins in “Sinners,” won the Oscar for best actor on Sunday night.

item.title: Paul Thomas Anderson Wins Best Director Oscar for ‘One Battle After Another’
item.description: It was his second win, after taking home the award for best adapted screenplay on Sunday.
item.dc:creator: Esther Zuckerman
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: Paul Thomas Anderson on the Oscars stage.

item.title: Autumn Durald Arkapaw Makes History as First Woman to Win Best Cinematography
item.description: The “Sinners” cinematographer was also the first woman of color to win that category. “I really want all the women in the room to stand up,” she said in her acceptance speech.
item.dc:creator: Melena Ryzik
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: Autumn Durald Arkapaw accepting her Oscar for best cinematography. She also thanked predecessors like Ellen Kuras and Rachel Morrison.

item.title: A Surprising Oscars Tie Is the Seventh in Academy Awards History
item.description: In 1969, Katharine Hepburn and Barbra Streisand each received an Oscar in the best actress category.
item.dc:creator: Livia Albeck-Ripka
item.media:credit: Associated Press
item.media:description: Barbra Streisand stood beside the director Anthony Harvey on the Oscars stage in 1969. Streisand was accepting the best actress award, which she shared with Katharine Hepburn (Harvey was accepting on Hepburn’s behalf).

item.title: ‘The Singers’ and ‘Two People Exchanging Saliva’ Tie for Oscar
item.description: “I’m not joking,” Kumail Nanjiani said while presenting the award for best live action short film.
item.dc:creator: Michaela Towfighi
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kumail Nanjiani announces one of the two winners for best live action short.

item.title: Barbra Streisand Pays Tribute to Robert Redford at 2026 Oscars
item.description: Streisand and Redford built a lengthy friendship after acting together in 1973’s “The Way We Were.”
item.dc:creator: Michaela Towfighi
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: “He was thoughtful and bold,” Barbra Streisand said of Robert Redford. “I called him an intellectual cowboy who blazed his own trail.”

item.title: Billy Crystal Leads 2026 Oscars Tribute to Rob and Michele Reiner
item.description: Billy Crystal led a tribute to the director several months after he and his wife were murdered.
item.dc:creator: Michaela Towfighi
item.media:credit: Frederic J. Brown/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Rob Reiner and his wife, Michele Singer Reiner, were killed in December.

item.title: Conan O’Brien Parodies ‘Weapons’ and Roasts Timothée Chalamet in 2026 Oscars Monologue
item.description: In his second turn as Oscars host, his monologue wasn’t as loopy as last year’s, but he displayed a range of jokes — nerdy, topical and physical.
item.dc:creator: Jason Zinoman
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: Conan O’Brien, dressed as Gladys in “Weapons,” led children on a chase through several movie scenes.

item.title: Sean Penn Wins Best Supporting Actor for ‘One Battle After Another’ but Skips Oscars
item.description: The actor has a history with the country’s president, Volodymyr Zelensky, and had been skipping awards ceremonies.
item.dc:creator: Jacob Bernstein
item.media:credit: Arnaud Finistre/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Sean Penn at a French festival in October.

item.title: All about the new Oscar for casting.
item.dc:creator: Esther Zuckerman

item.title: Amy Madigan Wins Oscar for Best Supporting Actress in ‘Weapons’
item.description: The actress won for her role as Aunt Gladys in Zach Cregger’s horror hit.
item.dc:creator: Emmanuel Morgan
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: Amy Madigan accepting the supporting actress Oscar for her role in “Weapons.”

item.title: Matt Berry will be this year’s ‘Voice of God.’
item.dc:creator: Maya Salam
item.media:credit: Jae C. Hong/Invision, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Matt Berry may be best known for “What We Do in the Shadows.”

item.title: Best picture nominees had mixed box office firepower.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes
item.media:credit: Sahiba Chawdhary/Reuters

item.title: Oscars Security Remains Tight Amid U.S.-Israel War With Iran
item.description: Top state and local officials have said there were no specific imminent threats despite the war unfolding in the Middle East.
item.dc:creator: Livia Albeck-Ripka, Emmanuel Morgan and Matt Stevens
item.media:credit: Blake Fagan/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Los Angeles police officers patrolling outside the Dolby Theater on Sunday.

item.title: Anti-ICE campaign takes to the streets near the ceremony.
item.dc:creator: Melena Ryzik
item.media:credit: Maremoto
item.media:description: “ICE Out” messages were projected in Los Angeles ahead of the Oscars.

item.title: How The New York Times Covers the Oscars
item.description: We’ll have TKTK Times reporters. Here’s how we plan to cover the presidential debate.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.media:credit: Jutharat Pinyodoonyachet for The New York Times
item.media:description: Roughly 60 journalists from The Times will contribute to live coverage.

item.title: What to expect on Sunday.
item.dc:creator: Kyle Buchanan
item.media:credit: Jutharat Pinyodoonyachet for The New York Times
item.media:description: After a well-received debut last year, Conan O’Brien, center, is back as the Oscars host.

item.title: Oscars 2026 Takeaways: ‘One Battle After Another’ Takes Top Honors
item.description: The film won six Academy Awards, including best director and best picture. “Sinners” received four, including for best screenplay and best actor.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: The longtime director Paul Thomas Anderson was first nominated for an Oscar in 1998, but he did not win until Sunday night.

item.title: How to watch the ceremony tonight.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.media:credit: Jutharat Pinyodoonyachet for The New York Times
item.media:description: Oscar gets shined up before the ceremony.

item.title: ‘Doctor Who’ Fans Have Fresh Chance to Time Travel With Found Episodes
item.description: Two unearthed episodes, which were discovered in film canisters wrapped in plastic bags among the possessions of a dead collector in England, were restored by BBC archivists.
item.dc:creator: Adeel Hassan
item.media:credit: BBC
item.media:description: From left, Nicholas Courtney as Bret Vyon, William Hartnell as the Doctor, Adrienne Hill as Katarina, and Peter Purves as Steven in a newly found “Dr. Who” episode called “The Devil’s Planet.”

item.title: Paul R. Ehrlich, Who Alarmed the World With ‘The Population Bomb,’ Dies at 93
item.description: His best-selling 1968 book, which forecast global famines, made him a leader of the environmental movement. But he faced criticism when his predictions proved premature.
item.dc:creator: Keith Schneider
item.media:credit: Sierra Club / Ballantine Books
item.media:description: “The Population Bomb” sold three million copies and turned Dr. Ehrlich into one of the global environmental movement’s most recognized leaders.

item.title: Paula Doress-Worters, an Author of ‘Our Bodies, Ourselves,’ Dies at 87
item.description: She wrote about postpartum depression when it was an unmentionable like abortion or birth control, and her research on her own suffering helped countless women.
item.dc:creator: Penelope Green

item.title: How to Watch the Oscars 2026: Start Time, Host and Streaming
item.description: Conan O’Brien is back for a second year as the host of the annual awards, which will again be available to stream on Hulu.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.media:credit: Jutharat Pinyodoonyachet for The New York Times

item.title: Jürgen Habermas Dies at 96; One of Postwar Germany’s Most Influential Thinkers
item.description: In dozens of books, he rejected postmodern cynicism about truth and reason, arguing that rational communication was the best way to redeem democratic society.
item.dc:creator: Gal Beckerman
item.media:credit: Sueddeutsche Zeitung Photo/Alamy Stock Photo
item.media:description: Jurgen Habermas at the University of Frankfurt in 1969. He was best known for introducing the idea of a “public sphere” in the early 1960s.

item.title: How Not to Become the ‘Dead Wife in a Movie’ Trope
item.description: You can’t win an Oscar if your death in Act I becomes your husband’s entire character arc. Our cartoonist has some plot-worthy ideas.
item.dc:creator: Michael Lukk Litwak

item.title: What to Watch for at the 2026 Oscars: Best Actor Race, Conan O’Brien and More
item.description: It will be a tight race for best picture and actor, while the In Memoriam segment looks to be supersized this year.
item.dc:creator: Kyle Buchanan
item.media:credit: Jutharat Pinyodoonyachet for The New York Times
item.media:description: After a well-received debut last year, Conan O’Brien, center, is back as the Oscars host.

item.title: Michael B. Jordan of ‘Sinners’ Is a Star. But Is He an Oscar-Worthy Actor?
item.description: Jordan has won our hearts and the box office. But there’s debate about his craft. After watching “Sinners” for the fourth time, I understand his talent.
item.dc:creator: Wesley Morris
item.media:credit: Warner Bros.
item.media:description: Michael B. Jordan plays twins in “Sinners”; he is nominated for best actor at the Academy Awards.

item.title: Prediction Markets? An 83% Chance That Oscars Pundits Hate Them.
item.description: Online wagering is all the rage. But the crowdsourced data generally doesn’t interest experts who have built a brand predicting Academy Awards races.
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens

item.title: Forget ‘Marty Supreme.’ Meet the Real Table Tennis Champion of New York.
item.description: Yasiris Ortiz, a 28-year-old star from the Bronx, is helping to bring free table tennis programing to children across the city.
item.dc:creator: Claire Fahy and Jonah Markowitz
item.media:credit: Jonah Markowitz for The New York Times

item.title: After DOGE Deposition Videos Go Viral, Judge Orders Them Taken Down
item.description: The videos had been posted on YouTube by groups who were suing to restore grants cut by Elon Musk’s Department of Government Efficiency.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Weiser and Jennifer Schuessler
item.media:credit: Jason Andrew for The New York Times
item.media:description: The building in Washington housing the National Endowment for the Humanities, where Elon Musk’s Department of Government Efficiency helped carry out sweeping cuts last year.

item.title: Margareta Magnusson,91, Dies; Wrote of Cleaning Up Before Dying
item.description: Her best-selling book encouraged people to tidy up homes and lives as death approached — as a gift for loved ones and to revisit memories.
item.dc:creator: Chris Kornelis
item.category: The Gentle Art of Swedish Death Cleaning: How to Make Your Loved Ones' Lives Easier and Your Own Life More Pleasant (Book)

item.title: At Dinners, Over Jokes With Comedians, Epstein Honed His Networking
item.description: In the years after his 2008 conviction for soliciting prostitution, Jeffrey Epstein rebuilt his reputation by hosting gatherings with leaders in all sorts of trades, including comedy.
item.dc:creator: Marc Tracy

item.title: Kneecap Gets Serious on Its New Album, ‘Fenian’
item.description: The gonzo Irish M.C.s first gained notice for their playful antics. After attracting global attention for their pro-Palestinian views, their new LP is more serious.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall and Max Miechowski
item.media:credit: Max Miechowski for The New York Times

item.title: Jack Harlow Was a Chart-Topping Rapper. He Doesn’t Want to Brag Anymore.
item.description: After smashes like “First Class” and “Lovin on Me,” the artist from Louisville, Ky., is making a true musical shift to intimate, hand-played R&B on his fourth album, “Monica.”
item.dc:creator: Joe Coscarelli, Jon Caramanica and Lucia Bell-Epstein For The New York Times
item.media:credit: Lucia Bell-Epstein for The New York Times

item.title: 5 New Movies Our Critics Are Talking About This Week
item.description: Whether you’re a casual moviegoer or an avid buff, our reviewers think these films are worth knowing about.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: 1,865 Coats in 15 Minutes: A Fine-Tuned Opera House Team
item.description: Each night, the coat check at the Deutsche Oper in Berlin deals with hundreds of jackets, no matter how fancy the audience dresses.
item.dc:creator: Christopher F. Schuetze and Gordon Welters
item.media:credit: Gordon Welters for The New York Times

item.title: How ‘Virgin River’ Became One of Netflix’s Biggest Hits
item.description: Despite the lack of big names or critical hype, the romance adaptation “Virgin River” has been one of Netflix’s biggest, most reliable successes.
item.dc:creator: Ashley Spencer and Alana Paterson
item.media:description: “Virgin River” is set in a small California town but is shot various towns and locations throughout southwest British Columbia.

item.title: Why Dogs Are a Writer’s Best Friend
item.description: Best-selling and award-winning authors spoke to us about how canines can spark creativity.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter
item.media:credit: Tony Luong for The New York Times
item.media:description: Alice Hoffman cradles her Tibetan terrier, Violet.

item.title: From ‘It Ends With Us’ to ‘Reminders of Him,’ How Colleen Hoover Captured Hollywood
item.description: The work of the novelist Colleen Hoover has become hot property in Hollywood. Here’s why studios clamor to adapt her books.
item.dc:creator: Alexis Soloski
item.media:credit: Jordan Strauss/Invision, via Associated Press
item.media:description: The author Colleen Hoover isn’t surprised her books translate well to the big screen. “I picture every book as a film as I’m writing it,” she said.

item.title: Behind the Scenes of the 2026 Oscar Best Picture Nominees: ‘Sinners,’ ‘Hamnet’ and More
item.description: In these videos, directors walked us through pivotal sequences from their 2026 Academy Award-nominated films.
item.dc:creator: Mekado Murphy

item.title: Grasping Esoteric Dance? It’s Child’s Play
item.description: A series of workshops that are part of the Dance Reflections festival help to demystify contemporary dance. There are classes for pros, nonprofessionals and even children.
item.dc:creator: Brian Seibert
item.media:credit: Rachel Papo for The New York Times
item.media:description: Three young students following instructions in a Trisha Brown class at the Van Cleef & Arpels Dance Reflections festival.

item.title: Late Night Unwraps ‘War’ vs. ‘Excursion’ vs. ‘Both’
item.description: The “Late Night” host Seth Meyers said President Trump is letting Americans “choose your own adventure” in the war with Iran.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)

item.title: Daniel Radcliffe Shines in ‘Every Brilliant Thing’ on Broadway
item.description: The actor’s fondness for the audience radiates outward in this delightful interactive play about naming and noticing the good in the world.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.media:credit: Sara Krulwich/The New York Times
item.media:description: Daniel Radcliffe, center right, with audience members in “Every Brilliant Thing” at the Hudson Theater in Manhattan.

item.title: ‘The Pitt’ Season 2, Episode 10 Recap: Invisible String
item.description: Family ties bind the characters together in this week’s episode, in ways both beautiful and suffocating.
item.dc:creator: Sean T. Collins
item.media:credit: Warrick Page/HBO Max
item.media:description: Dr. Mohan (Supriya Ganesh) had a panic attack, brought on partly by the incessant calling of her mother.
